sourceTitle: Tips to keep your team’s Notion pages up to date

questions.question: Which action enables you to receive notifications whenever a page you’re interested in is edited?

options.text: Toggle on “Follow this page” in the Updates tab
options.explanation: Following a page adds a checkmark next to Updates and sends you sidebar and email notifications for any changes.

options.text: Click the “Lock page” option
options.explanation: Locking a page prevents edits; it does not generate notifications.

options.text: Add a “Callout” block mentioning the page owner
options.explanation: A callout is for indicating ownership, not for subscribing to updates.

options.text: Create a new Slack channel for the page
options.explanation: While Slack integration can push updates, it requires additional steps; the direct way inside Notion is to follow the page.

questions.question: If you need to revert a page to how it looked 20 days ago, which plan must you be on to guarantee the version is still available?

options.text: Personal Free
options.explanation: Free plans only keep version history for 7 days.

options.text: Personal Pro or Team Plan
options.explanation: These plans retain page history for up to 30 days, covering a 20‑day‑old version.

options.text: Enterprise Plan only
options.explanation: Enterprise provides indefinite history, but it’s not required for a 20‑day window.

options.text: Any plan, as long as you manually export the page
options.explanation: Automatic version history depends on the plan; without the correct plan the version may be unavailable.

questions.question: Which database property would you use to quickly identify pages that haven’t been edited in the last month?

options.text: Created time
options.explanation: Created time shows when a page was first made, not its recent activity.

options.text: Last edited time
options.explanation: Last edited time timestamps the most recent edit, making it ideal for spotting stale pages.

options.text: Created by
options.explanation: Created by records the author, not the edit frequency.

options.text: Select property
options.explanation: A generic Select property doesn’t automatically track edit dates.

questions.question: When sorting a large database to surface the most frequently edited pages, which sort order should you apply to the “Last edited time” property?

options.text: Ascending
options.explanation: Ascending would place the oldest edits at the top, which is the opposite of what you want.

options.text: Descending
options.explanation: Descending puts the newest edits first, showing the most active pages at the top.

options.text: Random
options.explanation: Random sorting does not provide any meaningful insight into edit frequency.

options.text: No sort needed; filtering is enough
options.explanation: Sorting by Last edited time is the recommended method to rank pages by recency.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of creating an archive sub‑page for a top‑level page?

options.text: To permanently delete outdated content
options.explanation: Archiving keeps the content accessible; it does not delete it.

options.text: To keep old pages organized while still allowing reference
options.explanation: An archive sub‑page stores out‑dated pages for future reference without cluttering the main workspace.

options.text: To lock all pages inside it automatically
options.explanation: Archiving does not affect edit permissions; locking must be done separately.

options.text: To share the pages with external collaborators
options.explanation: Archiving is about internal organization, not external sharing.

questions.question: Which combination of steps correctly locks a page to prevent accidental edits?

options.text: Click the three‑dot menu (•••) → “Lock page”
options.explanation: Locking is performed via the three‑dot menu; once locked, no one can edit the page until it’s unlocked.

options.text: Toggle “Follow this page” in the Updates tab
options.explanation: Following subscribes to updates but does not restrict editing.

options.text: Add a Callout block mentioning the page owner
options.explanation: A callout indicates ownership but does not enforce edit protection.

options.text: Create a new database view with read‑only permissions
options.explanation: Changing view permissions affects only that view, not the underlying page itself.

questions.question: How can you set a reminder for a teammate to revisit a page in three months?

options.text: Type `@remind` followed by the date in a comment, then select the date picker
options.explanation: Using `@remind` with a date creates a reminder that notifies the mentioned teammate at the specified time.

options.text: Add a “Due Date” property to the page’s database
options.explanation: Due dates are for database items, not for ad‑hoc page reminders.

options.text: Lock the page for three months
options.explanation: Locking prevents edits; it does not schedule a future review.

options.text: Create a new Slack channel named after the page
options.explanation: Slack channels can be used for updates, but the specific Notion reminder feature uses `@remind`.

questions.question: What advantage does the “Database lock” provide over a regular page lock?

options.text: It prevents anyone from editing any content within the database, including individual rows
options.explanation: Database lock still allows editing of rows; it only restricts structural changes.

options.text: It stops users from changing views, properties, and select‑type values while still allowing row edits
options.explanation: Database lock locks the structure (views, properties) but permits editing of row content, which is the key distinction.

options.text: It automatically archives the entire database after 30 days
options.explanation: Archiving is a manual process; lock does not archive.

options.text: It disables all notifications for the database
options.explanation: Locking does not affect notification settings.

questions.question: When integrating Notion with Slack to push page updates, which of the following is NOT required?

options.text: Signing into Slack from the Notion integration prompt
options.explanation: You must authenticate with Slack to enable the connection.

options.text: Selecting the Slack channel where updates will be posted
options.explanation: Choosing a channel tells Notion where to send the notifications.

options.text: Enabling the “Connect Slack channel” toggle in the Updates menu
options.explanation: This toggle activates the integration for that specific page or database.

options.text: Creating a new Notion workspace for each Slack channel
options.explanation: A separate workspace is unnecessary; you can link any page/database to any Slack channel within the same workspace.

questions.question: Which method is recommended for visibly assigning ownership of a page to a teammate?

options.text: Add a Callout block at the top of the page and @‑mention the owner
options.explanation: A Callout with an @‑mention clearly displays who owns the page for anyone viewing it.

options.text: Change the page’s title to include the owner’s name
options.explanation: Renaming the title is not the standard way to indicate ownership and can clutter naming conventions.

options.text: Set the page’s permission to “Can view only” for everyone except the owner
options.explanation: Permissions control access, not visual ownership indication.

options.text: Add the owner’s email address in the page’s URL
options.explanation: Page URLs are system‑generated and cannot be manually edited to include emails.
